Q1: Honestly, my study routine was a bit all over the place this semester. I'd carve out about 29 hours a week for studying, but it wasn't always at the same times. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time making notes and rewriting key concepts in my own words. I'd usually study in longer blocks when I could focus, but my attendance in actual classes was pretty low because I found I could learn better on my own sometimes.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I have extracurriculars that take up time, so I'd have to shuffle things around. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, and other weeks I'd cram everything into a few intense sessions. Deadlines were the main thing that shaped when I studied, not a fixed schedule.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was something specific I needed to check—like an assignment deadline or new materials being posted. Sometimes I'd get a notification or remember that I hadn't looked at something in a while. I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse; there was always a purpose, even if it was just to download readings for later.
Q4: It did become routine at certain points, especially early in the semester when I was trying to stay on top of things. I'd check in after my extracurricular activities as a way to wind down. But honestly, that routine faded as the semester went on and I got busier with other things. The habit was there, but it wasn't super consistent.
Q5: The reading materials and any written resources were the most useful for me because of how I learn. I'd download PDFs and lecture notes and work through them on my own time. The videos were helpful too—I watched a lot of them—but I always preferred having something I could highlight and annotate. Discussion posts were okay, but I didn't engage with them as much.
Q6: I often skipped the discussion forums or only participated minimally. It's not that I didn't see value in them, but I felt like my time was better spent reading and writing notes. Some of the optional quizzes or activities felt like busywork when I was already understanding the material. I'd prioritize what directly helped me prepare for exams.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to what I'd be assessed on. If I could tell it would help me understand a concept better or prepare for an exam, I was in. But if something felt disconnected or like it was just there to fill space, I'd skip it. I needed to feel like I was actually learning, not just clicking through things.
Q8: Optional activities felt like extras that I could do if I had time, which I often didn't. Required ones had a different weight—there was accountability attached. I'd complete required tasks even if I wasn't motivated, but optional ones? Only if I was genuinely curious or had breathing room. The pressure was just different.
Q9: Oh, absolutely. A huge chunk of my studying happened offline with downloaded files and handwritten notes. I'd print things out or save them to my tablet and work through them without being connected to the LMS. I also used some external resources like YouTube videos for topics I found confusing. My LMS activity doesn't capture most of that.
Q10: There were so many times I'd be deep into studying from my printed notes or rewriting concepts in my notebook, and none of that showed up anywhere online. I'd spend hours working through problems or reading chapters, but because I wasn't logged in, it looked like I wasn't doing anything. My real engagement was mostly invisible.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access materials once, download them, and then never go back to the LMS for those specific resources. I preferred having everything saved locally so I could study without needing internet or getting distracted by notifications. One download could mean hours of studying that the system never sees.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, usually when stress from other areas of life piled up. Midway through the semester, I was juggling extracurriculars and felt my motivation dip. My attendance dropped and I found it harder to push myself to engage, even though I knew I should. The CBT session I had helped me reframe some of that, which was surprising actually.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one for me. Some weeks I was just exhausted from everything, and I'd log in but not really absorb anything. Competing responsibilities with my extracurriculars definitely affected how much energy I had for the LMS. When I was overwhelmed, I'd do the bare minimum online and focus on what I knew would help me pass.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was somewhere else. I'd technically be 'engaged' according to the system, but I wasn't actually learning. Those moments usually happened when I was tired or stressed and just trying to feel productive without really being productive.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. My assignment completion rate wasn't great because I'd convince myself I'd do better work later. Sometimes it was avoidance because I wasn't sure where to start, and other times I just didn't feel motivated enough. My resilience helped me bounce back, but the delay was real.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main driver for LMS activity. I'd ramp up my engagement right before something was due, which probably shows in my login patterns. Without a deadline looming, I was more likely to study offline at my own pace. The urgency of deadlines pushed me to interact with the platform more than anything else.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, but not always in a straightforward way. Good feedback motivated me to keep going, while critical feedback sometimes made me want to step back and process before re-engaging. I appreciated detailed comments because they helped me understand what to improve. But I didn't always rush back to the LMS after getting feedback.
Q18: The structure gave me a framework, but I often worked around it rather than within it. I'd look at what was required, figure out what I needed to do to succeed, and then do things my own way. The course was organized enough that I could pick and choose what to focus on. More flexibility would have suited my style even better.
Q19: Having materials available for download made engagement easier because I could study offline. What made it harder was when participation in discussions was expected—I'm not great at that kind of engagement. Also, when deadlines clustered together, it was overwhelming. Spreading things out more evenly would have helped me stay consistent.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to figure things out or feeling lost. They might be clicking around, watching videos, reading forums, but not feeling confident enough to submit work. Or maybe they're dealing with personal stuff that makes it hard to complete assignments even though they're trying to engage. Activity doesn't always equal output.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. My LMS activity was moderate, but I performed well because most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download everything, make my own notes, and prepare for exams without needing to be logged in constantly. The system doesn't capture that kind of independent learning, so it looks like less engagement than there actually was.
Q22: I think course logs can show patterns—like when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they spend on videos. But they miss the depth of engagement and all the offline work. They can't tell if someone is actually understanding the material or just going through the motions. Quantity of clicks doesn't equal quality of learning.
Q23: For me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's about being mentally present when I'm studying, asking myself questions, and connecting ideas. It's not about how many times I log in or how many posts I make. Real engagement is internal—it's about genuine learning and curiosity.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent with printed notes, rewriting concepts, or studying from my own summaries. It wouldn't capture the mental effort I put into understanding difficult topics or how I processed feedback emotionally before acting on it. It also wouldn't show that despite low motivation and medium stress, I pushed through because I'm resilient. That inner work is invisible.
